title: Major London hospitals disrupted by Synnovis ransomware attack
summary: A ransomware attack on Synnovis, an NHS pathology and diagnostics provider, severely disrupted operations at major London hospitals including Guy's, St Thomas', King's College, and Royal Brompton. Hundreds of operations and blood transfusions were cancelled. The NCSC coordinated the national response alongside NHS England.
full_text: A ransomware attack on Synnovis, a partnership providing pathology services to NHS trusts across London, has caused severe disruption to hospital operations including Guy's and St Thomas' NHS Foundation Trust, King's College Hospital, and the Royal Brompton. The Qilin ransomware group is believed to be responsible. Hundreds of blood transfusions and planned operations have been cancelled as a result. Patient-facing IT systems have been taken offline as a precaution. The NCSC has been notified and is working with NHS England to coordinate the national response. Incident response teams are actively investigating the scope of the compromise and whether patient data has been exfiltrated. This attack highlights the continued targeting of healthcare critical infrastructure by ransomware actors.

title: NCSC statement following reports of a Synnovis data breach
summary: The NCSC issued a statement confirming that it is supporting NHS England and Synnovis following the ransomware attack and subsequent data breach. The Qilin ransomware group published nearly 400GB of patient data online. The NCSC urged healthcare organisations to review their cyber resilience.
full_text: The National Cyber Security Centre has issued a statement confirming it is actively supporting NHS England and Synnovis following the ransomware attack that disrupted London hospitals and the subsequent publication of patient data by the Qilin ransomware group. Approximately 400GB of patient data, including blood test results and patient records, was published on a darknet site. The NCSC is working with law enforcement partners including the NCA and Metropolitan Police on the criminal investigation. Healthcare organisations are urged to review their cyber resilience, ensure backups are tested and offline, and review their third-party supplier risk. Patients are being contacted where their data is believed to have been compromised.

title: Threat Actors Chained Vulnerabilities in Ivanti Cloud Service Applications
summary: CISA and the FBI published a joint advisory documenting active exploitation of chained vulnerabilities in Ivanti Cloud Service Appliances, including CVE-2024-8963, CVE-2024-9380, and CVE-2024-8190. Attackers achieved remote code execution and lateral movement within victim networks. Organisations are urged to apply patches immediately.
full_text: CISA and the FBI have published a joint cybersecurity advisory warning of active exploitation of chained vulnerabilities in Ivanti Cloud Service Appliances (CSA). Threat actors are exploiting CVE-2024-8963 (path traversal, CVSS 9.4), CVE-2024-9380 (OS command injection), and CVE-2024-8190 (OS command injection) in combination to achieve unauthenticated remote code execution and lateral movement within victim networks. Multiple threat actors including suspected nation-state groups are exploiting these vulnerabilities to install web shells, harvest credentials, and conduct further intrusion activity. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies must apply mitigations per CISA's BOD 22-01. All organisations running affected Ivanti CSA versions should patch immediately and hunt for indicators of compromise detailed in the advisory.

title: Volt Typhoon targets US critical infrastructure with living-off-the-land techniques
summary: Microsoft Threat Intelligence revealed that Volt Typhoon, a Chinese state-sponsored actor active since mid-2021, has been conducting stealthy espionage against US critical infrastructure including communications, manufacturing, utilities, and transportation sectors. The campaign relies exclusively on living-off-the-land techniques and legitimate system binaries to evade detection.
full_text: Microsoft Threat Intelligence has identified Volt Typhoon, a Chinese state-sponsored actor, conducting stealthy post-compromise espionage against US critical infrastructure organisations including in the communications, manufacturing, utility, transportation, construction, maritime, government, IT, and education sectors. The actor has been active since mid-2021 and operates primarily in Guam and elsewhere in the United States. Critically, Volt Typhoon relies almost exclusively on living-off-the-land (LotL) techniques using built-in Windows tools such as wmic, ntdsutil, netsh, and PowerShell to blend in with legitimate activity, making detection extremely difficult. Microsoft assesses that this pre-positioning is intended to enable disruption of critical communications infrastructure between the US and Asia in the event of future crises. CISA, NSA, FBI and international partners jointly issued guidance alongside this disclosure.

title: GitHub Actions Supply Chain Attack: Targeted Attack on Coinbase Expanded to tj-actions/changed-files
summary: Unit 42 traced a supply chain attack that began as a targeted intrusion against Coinbase via a compromised SpotBugs maintainer token. Attackers pivoted to inject malicious code into the widely-used tj-actions/changed-files GitHub Action, ultimately exposing CI/CD secrets across more than 23,000 repositories.
full_text: Unit 42 researchers have published a detailed analysis of a sophisticated software supply chain attack that began with a targeted intrusion against Coinbase. Attackers initially compromised a SpotBugs GitHub Actions workflow by obtaining a personal access token belonging to a SpotBugs maintainer. They then used this access to inject malicious code into the tj-actions/changed-files and reviewdog GitHub Actions, two widely-used open source packages. The malicious code exfiltrated CI/CD secrets, environment variables, and GitHub tokens from repositories running these actions. The attack affected over 23,000 repositories before being detected and remediated by the maintainers. This incident underscores the systemic risk posed by transitive dependencies in modern software supply chains and the importance of pinning GitHub Actions to specific commit hashes.

title: ENISA Threat Landscape 2024
summary: ENISA's annual threat landscape report identifies threats against availability (DDoS), ransomware, and data-related attacks as the EU's top three concerns. The report documents major threat trends across sectors and notes increasing activity from state-sponsored actors in Russia, China, North Korea, and Iran.
full_text: The European Union Agency for Cybersecurity has published its annual ENISA Threat Landscape report for 2024. The report analyses thousands of publicly reported cybersecurity incidents to identify the top threats facing EU member states and organisations. Threats against availability, primarily distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks, rank first for the second consecutive year. Ransomware retains its position as a top three threat, with double-extortion attacks increasing significantly. Data-related attacks including data breaches and data theft complete the top three. The report also highlights the growing intersection of cyber and physical attacks, increasing use of AI by both threat actors and defenders, and the continued targeting of critical infrastructure sectors by state-sponsored actors primarily from Russia, China, North Korea, and Iran. The energy, transport, banking, and healthcare sectors remain the most targeted.

title: FBI Confirms Lazarus Hackers Were Behind $1.5B Bybit Crypto Heist
summary: The FBI formally attributed the theft of $1.5 billion from cryptocurrency exchange Bybit to North Korea's Lazarus Group (TraderTraitor), making it the largest crypto heist on record. Attackers compromised a Safe{Wallet} developer machine to manipulate a routine cold-wallet transfer, then laundered funds via mixers and cross-chain bridges.
full_text: The Federal Bureau of Investigation has formally attributed the February 21 2025 theft of $1.5 billion in cryptocurrency from Bybit to North Korea's Lazarus Group, also tracked as TraderTraitor. The attack represents the largest cryptocurrency theft on record. Investigators determined that attackers compromised a developer machine belonging to Safe{Wallet}, a multi-signature wallet provider used by Bybit, allowing them to manipulate a routine cold-wallet transfer. The stolen funds were rapidly laundered through thousands of blockchain addresses, using cryptocurrency mixers and cross-chain bridges to obscure the trail. North Korea's Lazarus Group has now stolen an estimated $6 billion in cryptocurrency since 2017, primarily to fund the regime's weapons programmes. The FBI published cryptocurrency addresses associated with the operation to assist industry partners in blocking further laundering.

title: Ivanti warns of new Connect Secure flaw used in zero-day attacks
summary: Ivanti disclosed CVE-2025-0282, a critical stack-based buffer overflow in Connect Secure, Policy Secure, and ZTA Gateways actively exploited as a zero-day. Attackers installed malware on appliances before patches were available. CISA added the CVE to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalogue the same day.
full_text: Ivanti has disclosed CVE-2025-0282, a critical stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability in Ivanti Connect Secure (formerly Pulse Secure), Policy Secure, and ZTA Gateways with a CVSS score of 9.0. The vulnerability was being actively exploited as a zero-day prior to the patch release, with attackers installing SPAWNCHIMERA malware on compromised appliances. Mandiant and Volexity independently identified exploitation in the wild. Ivanti urged administrators to run the Integrity Checker Tool to detect potential compromise and perform a factory reset on any affected appliance before upgrading. CISA added CVE-2025-0282 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalogue on the same day and ordered federal agencies to apply mitigations within 48 hours. Nation-state actors are suspected given the sophistication of the post-exploitation tooling.

title: Email Attacks Drive Record Cybercrime Losses in 2024
summary: Proofpoint's analysis of the FBI's 2024 Internet Crime Report found cybercrime losses hit a record $16.6 billion, with email the dominant attack vector. Phishing-related losses surged 274% year-on-year and business email compromise cost victims $2.77 billion across more than 21,000 incidents.
full_text: Proofpoint's analysis of the FBI's 2024 Internet Crime Report documents record-breaking cybercrime losses of $16.6 billion, a 33% increase on 2023. Email remains the primary attack vector across the largest loss categories. Phishing and related social engineering attacks drove losses up 274% year-on-year, reflecting the rapid adoption of AI-generated lures and increasingly sophisticated credential harvesting infrastructure. Business email compromise (BEC) was the second-costliest category with $2.77 billion in losses across 21,489 complaints, targeting finance, legal, and insurance organisations. Investment fraud, predominantly crypto fraud, topped the loss table at $6.57 billion. Proofpoint urges organisations to enforce DMARC, deploy multi-factor authentication across all email accounts, and invest in regular security awareness training given the continued effectiveness of phishing as an initial access vector.

title: SentinelOne Intelligence Brief: Iranian Cyber Activity Outlook
summary: SentinelOne assesses that Iranian state-aligned actors are likely to intensify offensive cyber operations targeting US and Israeli critical infrastructure, drawing on Iran's pattern of retaliatory campaigns. Expected tactics include destructive wiper deployment, precision espionage against defence networks, and probing attacks on industrial control systems.
full_text: SentinelOne's threat intelligence team has published an intelligence brief assessing the near-term outlook for Iranian state-sponsored cyber operations. The assessment concludes that Iranian actors are likely to intensify offensive operations against US and Israeli critical infrastructure in the current geopolitical environment. Iran's primary state-aligned threat groups — including APT33 (Refined Kitten), APT34 (OilRig), and Charming Kitten — have demonstrated the capability and willingness to deploy destructive wiper malware against energy, water, and defence sector targets. SentinelOne expects continued precision espionage operations against defence contractors and government networks, combined with probing attacks against industrial control systems and operational technology environments. Organisations in targeted sectors are advised to increase monitoring, implement network segmentation between IT and OT environments, and review incident response plans.

title: Microsoft April 2025 Patch Tuesday fixes exploited zero-day, 134 flaws
summary: Microsoft's April 2025 Patch Tuesday addressed 134 vulnerabilities including one actively exploited zero-day, CVE-2025-29824, a use-after-free elevation of privilege flaw in the Windows Common Log File System Driver exploited in ransomware attacks. CISA added the CVE to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalogue the same day.
full_text: Microsoft's April 2025 Patch Tuesday addressed 134 security vulnerabilities across Windows, Office, Edge, Azure, and Visual Studio. One vulnerability, CVE-2025-29824, is confirmed as actively exploited in the wild. The flaw is a use-after-free elevation of privilege vulnerability in the Windows Common Log File System (CLFS) Driver that allows a local attacker to gain SYSTEM-level privileges. Microsoft and CISA confirmed it has been exploited in ransomware attacks against organisations in the US, Spain, Venezuela, and Saudi Arabia. Eleven vulnerabilities are rated Critical, covering remote code execution in Windows Remote Desktop Services, LDAP, and Office. Security teams should prioritise patching CLFS and internet-exposed Windows Server instances. CISA added CVE-2025-29824 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalogue and ordered federal agencies to patch within 3 days.
